data.title: Microsoft, Amazon seen 'standing out' from strong cloud spending, Jefferies says
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) are “standing out” in a recent survey showing strong cloud computing spending for the rest of 2026, Jefferies said.

“Cloud spend will modestly accelerate to 10.1% in '26 vs. 9.6% in '25 based on survey data, vs. our previous cloud survey, which implied a slight deceleration,” Jefferies analysts wrote in a note to clients. “~95% of respondents expect their cloud budgets to grow y/y in '26. 53% expect their cloud budgets to grow by single digits, while 43% anticipate a double-digit increase. Only 3% of survey respondents anticipate a marginal decrease in '26 cloud spend, consistent w/our previous survey.”

Additionally, the survey, which interviewed 40 U.S. Chief Information Officers, showed that more IT workloads are expected to shift towards the cloud in the next couple of years. Eighty-five percent of respondents said that more than half of workloads would be on the cloud by the end of 2027, up from 75% of respondents. And 53% of respondents expect cloud workloads to be more than 80% by 2027, “a significant step-up from 25% seeing those levels today,” the analysts added.

The survey also showed the major cloud service providers—Azure, AWS, and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) Cloud—as being the biggest winners from AI adoption, a consistent figure from past surveys.  

Other takeaways

The survey also provided other takeaways, including the fact that Microsoft's Azure extended its lead over AWS as the preferred cloud vendor for the group of CIOs.

“Azure is the primary Cloud provider for 55% of respondents in this survey vs. AWS at 28%, a 27pt gap that has widened from just 7pts in Dec '25 (Azure 45% / AWS 38%),” the analysts wrote. “Spend allocation tells the same story, w/ the gap widening to 17pts (Azure 46% / AWS 29%) from 3pts last survey (Azure 40% / AWS 37%). While the directional widening on both metrics signals Azure momentum, we believe all 3 hyperscalers can benefit as AI demand continues to exceed supply.”

Additionally, 68% of respondents said they expect Azure spending to increase over the next 24 months, while only 45% said they expect AWS spending to increase and just 25% for Google Cloud.

“Notably, GCP's net spending score for CY26 has slipped to 35% from 52% in Dec '25, though we'd flag the smaller sample size (35% of CIOs marked GOOG as not applicable),” the analysts explained. “Directionally, the read still signals GCP's continued enterprise push as the dark horse in cloud.”

data.title: Stellantis North American June vehicle sales increase 10%
data.description:
• Stellantis (STLA) sold 634,187 vehicles during the first six months of 2026, an increase of 5% year-over-year led by increased demand for the Jeep Grand Wagoneer.
  • Sales for the month of June alone were up 10% and up 6% for the second quarter compared to the same period last year.
  • The sales trajectory sets the company up to achieve its ambitious FaSTLAne goal of 25% revenue growth and 35% volume through 2030 with a 50% increase in market coverage.
  • After bottoming out at a 6-year low on Monday, Stellantis (STLA) shares are in the green for a second day.

data.title: NETSTREIT leads most-shorted REIT stocks above $2B; JAN sees fewer bets
data.description:
Mid- and large-cap U.S. REITs with market capitalizations above $2B are showing a clear split in investor sentiment, with retail, healthcare, hotel, and office landlords attracting the highest short interest, while industrial, data center, and tower REITs remain among the least shorted names.

NETSTREIT (NTST) topped the list of the most heavily shorted mid- and large-cap REIT stocks, with 33.72% of its shares sold short.

The retail REIT was followed by Medical Properties Trust (MPT) at 22.00% and Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) at 18.29%.

The list is largely dominated by retail, healthcare, hotel, and office REITs, segments that continue to face pressure from tenant demand uncertainty, higher financing costs, and concerns over property valuations.

Top 5 most heavily shorted mid- and large-cap REIT stocks (market cap above $2B)

  • NETSTREIT (NTST) – Short interest: 33.72%

  • Medical Properties Trust (MPT) – Short interest: 22.00%

  • Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) – Short interest: 18.29%

  • Douglas Emmett (DEI) – Short interest: 17.10%

  • Acadia Realty Trust (AKR) – Short interest: 16.17%

On the other hand, Janus Living (JAN) was the least shorted mid- and large-cap REIT with short interest of just 1.21%, followed by Prologis (PLD) at 1.71%.

Top 5 least shorted mid- and large-cap REIT stocks (market cap above $2B)

  • Janus Living (JAN) – Short interest: 1.21%

  • Prologis (PLD) – Short interest: 1.71%

  • LXP Industrial Trust (LXP) – Short interest: 1.91%

  • Equinix (EQIX) – Short interest: 1.92%

  • American Tower (AMT) – Short interest: 1.92%

Here are some ETFs with exposure to these REIT sectors and property types: (VNQ), (XLRE), (IYR), (SCHH), (ICF), (REET), and (MORT).

data.title: Arbor Realty tops most-shorted small-cap REITs list as bears target mortgage names
data.description:
Small-cap U.S. REITs with market capitalizations below $2B are showing a wide gap in investor sentiment, with mortgage and office landlords attracting the highest bearish bets, while healthcare, residential, and data center REITs remain among the least shorted names.

Arbor Realty Trust (ABR) topped the list of the most heavily shorted small-cap REIT stocks, with 22.53% of its shares sold short.

The mortgage REIT was followed by JBG SMITH Properties (JBGS) at 17.11% and Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) at 13.01%.

The list is largely dominated by mortgage and office REITs, two segments that continue to face pressure from higher interest rates, refinancing costs, and slower demand for office space.

Top 5 most heavily shorted small-cap REIT stocks (market cap below $2B)

  • Arbor Realty Trust (ABR) – Short interest: 22.53%

  • JBG SMITH Properties (JBGS) – Short interest: 17.11%

  • Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) – Short interest: 13.01%

  • Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP) – Short interest: 11.95%

  • Invesco Mortgage Capital (IVR) – Short interest: 9.50%

On the other hand, investors appear significantly more optimistic toward healthcare, residential, industrial, and data center REITs, which reported some of the lowest short interest levels.

Strawberry Fields REIT (STRW) was the least shorted small-cap REIT with short interest of just 0.67%, followed by BRT Apartments (BRT) at 0.91%.

Top 5 least shorted small-cap REIT stocks (market cap below $2B)

  • Strawberry Fields REIT (STRW) – Short interest: 0.67%

  • BRT Apartments (BRT) – Short interest: 0.91%

  • Blackstone Digital Infrastructure (BXDC) – Short interest: 1.00%

  • Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) – Short interest: 1.09%

  • One Liberty Properties (OLP) – Short interest: 1.16%

Here are some ETFs with exposure to these REIT sectors and property types: Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE), iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR), Schwab U.S. REIT ETF (SCHH), iShares Cohen & Steers REIT ETF (ICF), iShares Global REIT ETF (REET), and VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT).

data.title: U.S. stocks mixed, Dow rises to record at the start of the second half of the year
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes were mixed on Wednesday, with the Nasdaq Composite ((COMP:IND)) struggling to stay above water due to technology stocks declining amid ongoing volatility.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.2%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.5% and reached a new all-time high.

Among the worst decliners within tech were Micron (MU) -8.8%, Sandisk (SNDK) -9.3%, Nvidia (NVDA) -1.3%, and Broadcom (AVGO) -2.3%.

Meta Platforms (META), on the other hand, jumped more than 9% after Bloomberg reported it is building a cloud business for excess artificial intelligence compute.

On the economic side, the ISM Manufacturing PMI fell more than expected in June, while PMI Manufacturing unexpectedly dropped.

In addition, construction spending ticked up in May, slightly lower than expected, and the U.S. private sector added 98,000 jobs in June, missing the +117,000 consensus, according to ADP.

Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh said on Wednesday that the Fed will “chart a new course” on forward guidance, contrasting with his predecessor's practice of indicating which way policymakers were leaning in upcoming meetings.

Bond yields were mostly muted. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was flat at 4.47%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 4 basis points to 4.16%.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were lower at $68, while Brent (CO1:COM) was $71 per barrel.

data.title: Midday Need to Know: Meta's AI cloud push, Kroger deal & more
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes were mixed on Wednesday, with the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) struggling to stay above water due to technology stocks declining amid ongoing volatility.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.1%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was -0.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.3%.

Now, here are four news stories that broke in the morning to watch out for:

Meta Platforms plans cloud business: Shares of Meta (META)  jumped more than 10% in early trading after Bloomberg reported the company is forming a business unit called Meta Compute to generate revenue from excess artificial intelligence computing power. The plans include selling access to various AI models hosted on Meta's infrastructure, similar to Amazon Web Services' Bedrock approach, as well as selling raw computing capacity akin to neocloud providers. 

Kroger acquires Giant Eagle: Kroger (KR)  announced the acquisition of Giant Eagle, one of the largest privately held grocery chains in the U.S., for $1.65 billion, comprising $1.25 billion in cash and approximately $400 million in assumed liabilities. Giant Eagle generates roughly $9 billion in annual sales across 197 supermarkets and 11 standalone pharmacies in northern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, West Virginia, Maryland, and Indiana. Kroger CEO Greg Foran called the acquisition a strategic fit that expands the company's reach into attractive adjacent markets. 

Federal Reserve Chair reviews economic data: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh  outlined his aspirations for the five task forces established last month to review the central bank's practices, stating he hopes to leverage new technologies for real-time economic analysis within nine to 12 months. Speaking at the European Central Bank Forum on Central Banking, Warsh said he wants the Fed to move beyond relying solely on government agency data with "mismeasurement problems" and outdated surveys. 

Elizabeth Warren calls for crypto limits: Senator Elizabeth Warren  urged stronger crypto legislation ahead of a key Senate floor vote, demanding measures to prevent President Donald Trump and his family from continuing to profit from digital asset ventures. Trump's latest financial disclosure showed he earned more than $1.4 billion from cryptocurrency ventures in 2025, including nearly $800 million from World Liberty Financial, the crypto company he co-founded with his sons. Former Trump White House lawyer Ty Cobb told CNN the situation represents "the greatest onslaught of corruption in the history of mankind in the past 18 months."

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: MU, COST, GOOG, AMZN
data.description:
Recent analyst actions from Seeking Alpha feature notable upgrades for Micron Technology (MU) and Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST), driven by record earnings and macroeconomic tailwinds respectively. On the downside, Alphabet (GOOG) faces a downgrade amid concerns over massive capital expenditures and margin compression, while Amazon (AMZN) draws caution due to significant valuation premiums despite strong fundamentals..

Upgrades

  • Micron Technology (MU): Upgrade to Buy by Louis Gerard. The analyst points to record-breaking Q3 results and Strategic Customer Agreements that lock in significant capacity, providing downside protection in future downcycles.

    "The reason for this staggering quarter is not simply a better cycle but rather the scale of Strategic Customer Agreements, as Micron disclosed 16 legally binding take-or-pay contracts running from 2026 through 2030. All of these will lock in roughly 20% of DRAM and 33% of NAND capacity, carrying around $100B of remaining performance obligations that are backstopped by about $22B of customer collateral, of which around $18B is cash already on deposit."

  • Costco Wholesale (COST): Upgrade to Short-term Buy by APAC Research. The analyst cites high fuel prices and inflation as key drivers pushing consumers toward value-seeking behavior, benefiting Costco's membership model.

    "Inflation outside of food has also accelerated. This could be a boon for Costco as more and more customers turn to the company to save money. Once members are locked in with a membership, many will likely stay even after fuel costs come down."

Downgrades

  • Alphabet (GOOG): Downgrade to Hold by Esxeleryn Analytics. The analyst warns that Alphabet's aggressive AI infrastructure spending will trigger near-term margin compression and a significant depreciation burden.

    "On the downside, the main factor is the CapEx/depreciation gravity well and drop in asset turnover. To add more here, Alphabet’s infrastructure buildout is forming an adverse state for its forward income statements...If macroeconomic conditions tighten, Alphabet will be trapped with a huge fixed-cost depreciation drag that may lead to large GAAP margin compression."

  • Amazon.com (AMZN): Valuation Concerns Raised by Hunting Alphas. While fundamentally bullish on AWS and e-commerce growth, the analyst flags a steep valuation premium that creates notable overvaluation risk.

    "The key driver of this impressive growth is quite simply massive enterprise demand for AI infrastructure and cloud computing...Besides providing a revenue boost, Amazon's custom chips, such as Trainium, are also expected to result in capex and opex cost savings."

data.title: Nissan looks to cut costs on Mexico-built models as U.S. tariffs weigh
data.description:
Nissan Motor (NSANY) is working to reduce costs on vehicles built in Mexico to offset the impact of U.S. tariffs that have made some models harder to sell in the United States, Bloomberg News reported on Wednesday, citing an interview with Chief Executive Ivan Espinosa.

Espinosa said 25% tariffs on vehicles imported from Mexico were making part of Nissan's lineup more difficult to sell in the U.S. market, where affordability remains under pressure.

The Japanese automaker is seeking to make its Mexico-built vehicles, including the Sentra sedan and Kicks crossover, more competitive while retaining production in Mexico to benefit from lower labor costs.

Nissan (NSANY) has previously said tariffs on the Sentra and Kicks add about $2,500 to $3,000 to the cost of each vehicle.

Espinosa also said Nissan (NSANY) would continue investing in U.S. manufacturing, adding that producing vehicles locally remained a sensible strategy despite the weaker yen. The company produced about 60% of the vehicles it sold in the United States locally last year, up from roughly 45% at the beginning of 2025.

Shares of the company were up about 3% in the afternoon trading.

data.description:
BTIG analysts selected their favorite stock picks over a 12-month investment period within the small- and mid-cap space for the second half of the year.

These are BTIG’s highest conviction investment ideas with smaller caps that are buy-rated by its research team.

“As we look ahead to 2H’26, we see evidence that the broadening can continue,” analyst said. “Financials (XLF) remain strong across banks (KBE), REITs (XLRE), and insurance (KIE), (IAK). Biotech (XBI), (BBH), (IBB) is coming on strong as IBB breaks out of a five-year base. We have seen Mag7 (MAGS) quickly roll over, and while they can probably stabilize, we continue to think the semi/AI trade is vulnerable to a more meaningful unwind lower.”

data.title: Fedspeak hits most hawkish level since late 2023
data.description:
The tone of Federal Reserve officials has shifted significantly in recent weeks, with Bloomberg Economics’ proprietary Fedspeak model having reached its most hawkish level since the end of 2023.

The index, which tracks and quantifies the underlying sentiment of speeches and statements made by Fed policymakers, recently surged to 5.83 by the end of June 2026, according to a chart circulated by Charles Schwab's Kevin Gordon.

This steep upward trajectory marks a stark reversal from the deeply dovish territory the index occupied through much of 2024 and early 2025. 

As such, the data indicate that central bank officials are increasingly signaling a preference for restrictive monetary policy. For example, Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said earlier in the week that higher interest rates may be needed to bring inflation to the monetary authority's 2% target. But she's not necessarily supporting a rate hike in the July meeting.

Markets, too, are leaning more hawkish, with traders on prediction marketplace Kalshi seeing a greater than 50% chance the Fed will increase rates this year.

Meanwhile, Fed Chair Kevin Warsh said Wednesday that expectations of inflation over the last four weeks have come down, and inflation risks have come down. He added that anyone who expected the Fed to be content with inflation above its target "will be disappointed."
